name: Genderuwo
description: The Genderuwo is a prominent demon or spirit in Javanese mythology, known for its large size, hairy body, and mischievous or malevolent nature. Often described as a shapeshifter, the Genderuwo is said to inhabit damp, dark places like old buildings, large trees, and abandoned areas. It is believed to possess the ability to take on human forms, frequently impersonating family members or loved ones to trick and deceive people. Genderuwos are often associated with causing disturbances, creating illusions, and even impregnating women. They are considered powerful beings capable of influencing human lives, and are often feared and respected within Javanese communities. Stories of the Genderuwo serve as warnings against venturing into dangerous or deserted locations, and emphasize the importance of respecting the spirit world. The creature represents untamed nature and the potential dangers lurking in the shadows. Rituals and offerings are sometimes made to appease or ward off Genderuwos, reflecting the deep-seated belief in their existence and influence.
subtitle: The Hairy Javanese Demon
appearance: The Genderuwo is typically described as a large, muscular creature covered in thick, black hair. Its eyes are often red or glowing, and it possesses long, sharp claws and teeth. Some accounts describe it as having a foul odor. While it can shapeshift, its true form is often said to be grotesque and intimidating.
habitat: Genderuwos are believed to inhabit damp, dark places such as old buildings, large trees (especially banyan trees), caves, graveyards, and abandoned areas. They are particularly drawn to places with a history of tragedy or violence.
behavior: Genderuwos are known for their mischievous and sometimes malevolent behavior. They enjoy playing tricks on humans, creating illusions, and causing disturbances. They are also believed to be sexually aggressive, with some accounts claiming they impregnate women. Genderuwos are often associated with feelings of unease, fear, and paranoia.

keyMyths.title: The Impersonation
keyMyths.description: A Genderuwo takes the form of a husband to trick his wife, resulting in a child with supernatural abilities. This myth warns against trusting appearances and highlights the dangers of the spirit world.

keyMyths.title: The Haunted Tree
keyMyths.description: Villagers avoid a specific banyan tree because it is believed to be inhabited by a Genderuwo. The tree becomes a symbol of fear and serves as a warning against disturbing the creature's domain.

sources.author: Priyadi, Sugeng
sources.description: A compilation of Javanese myths and legends.
sources.text: A compilation of Javanese myths and legends.

sources.author: Endraswara, Suwardi
sources.description: An exploration of the Javanese belief in ghosts and spirits.
